item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not really down with the ethical dilemma that's presented here. It's such a straightforward examination of the idea that it doesn't inspire any actual consideration; there's a clear bad guy and a clear good guy, and an uninspiring anomaly in between.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is trying to beat me over the head with pathos. The first rule of writing emotional feel-good articles is that you shouldn't write an article with the intent of being feel-good, you should try to tell a story and let the emotions come out naturally through character interaction. That's not whats happening here. Even from the interview, you hammer in the details that this is a poor village and priest who uses the anomaly to feed his people, altruistic, etc. The Conproc revisions are super heavy-handed in their elaboration of it.

Given the fact that he lost his means to do that, as well as the fabricated drug bust against him, his life is in a significantly lower spot than before. Giving SCP-5246 back to him will restore his life, and the people’s faith in him.

I know that the Foundation can be cold, but taking a priest's method to feed the ones around him is cruel. We're not cruel, right?

Like, you could've just put a log of the suggested revision without the heartfelt explanation why, that just ruins any emotional weight it had. This ties back to the first rule; appearing like you're trying to pull the reader's heartstrings will kill any chance you have of doing so, because then they're aware of it.
I don't really have much to say on the front of the interview, but I have to note that the article also has a big ol' case of note-at-the-end syndrome. Not in that the note explains whats going on, but it's…. it's like a TV episode ending and then one of the characters turns to the camera and goes "And the moral of the story is….". It's just corny, and undercuts the effect that the reader should naturally come to without having one of the characters explain it to them directly.

item.title: Number
item.content:encoded: I edited a few cases of "5426" to "5246"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Something small that I noticed when reading. There are two spots where you forgot to replace SCP-XXXX with SCP-5246. I listed them below. Otherwise, amazing article. I really enjoyed it. +1

Dizon shows a time-lapse video of SCP-XXXX. Video was taken when SCP-XXXX was in Foundation custody.

Cornez: Inigo. Yes. I do remember her mentioning something like that. Not familiar though, SCP-XXXX is Safe right? I usually only check the more dangerous ones.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for your comment! I too like a good pot of rice.

Also, I didn't know that paella wasn't that common in the west! Thanks for letting me know about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you so much for this comment. Looking back, I feel like I focused too much on the interview with the priest. This helped me look into the ending and edit it quite a bit. Added a meeting between the Director and the two Researchers in the Incident collapsible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. The story is well paced and kept me interested throughout. The tone is on point. I like the dialogue in the interviews, the characters in them seem real and distinct from one another. This object sounds like one I'd want to have. There's nothing better than a good pot of rice.

I found a couple places where the wording sounds a bit off that could be fixed by adding a word or two.

Foundation agents embedded in local governance were able to confirm the object’s effects, retrieve the object, and issue amnestics to the police

'issue amnestics to the police' sounds a bit weird to me. I'd suggest changing it to something like 'issue amnestics to the police officers involved'.

Dizon: Greetings Father Pedro. I am Detective Dizon. My department found the police operation at your residence suspicious, and have sent me to interview your side of the story.

'interview your side of the story' again sounds a bit weird to me. I'd suggesting changing it to something like 'interview you for your side of the story'.

A couple parts where you might want to make small clarifications are.

Paella

You might want to foot note this too. I live in the US and paella isn't a dish that everyone will know.

Foundation personnel looked into the records of the police station and were able to identify the target of the drug raid, hereby designated as PoI-5246-1.

You might want to add something like 'hereby a person of interested designated PoI-5246-1.' PoI isn't, at least in my experience, a very common abbreviation in SCP articles. You might want to indicate what it means for people who don't know.

Great article. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This doesn't quite hit it with me. I really appreciate the details given with the initial discovery and the following interview log. That kind of misfortune and display of police corruption really makes me feel for Father Pedro, especially since he gives into his community. This kind of unfairness is tangible and sticks well.

However, I think the issue I have with this lies in the latter half. There's a little bit of struggle with the conpoc revision proposal, but to have the item be given up with so little resistance makes it seem… trivial, I guess. If it was just a case of pitching the proposal to the site director, it's a wonder that Dizon didn't do that sooner.

I think I would have personally preferred a bigger "fight for what's right" here. While I can get behind a Foundation that does something obviously good for once, this feels as if it resolves itself almost too easily.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First SCP by me.
